techniques.name: Boiling Water
techniques.subtitle: The gold standard of purification

steps.instruction: Bring water to a rolling boil.
steps.helpDetail: A rolling boil means large bubbles breaking the surface continuously — not just small bubbles on the bottom.

steps.instruction: Maintain the boil for 1 minute (3 minutes above 6,500 ft).
steps.helpDetail: Boiling kills bacteria, viruses, and parasites. At altitude, water boils at a lower temperature so extra time compensates.

steps.instruction: Let cool before drinking.
steps.helpDetail: Store in a clean container. Do not add untreated water to the clean water. Boiled water tastes flat — pour between containers to add air.

techniques.name: Iodine Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Chemical purification for clear water

steps.instruction: Add 5 drops of 2% iodine tincture per quart of clear water.
steps.helpDetail: Double the dose for cloudy water. Triple for very cold water (below 40°F).

steps.instruction: Wait 30 minutes before drinking.
steps.helpDetail: Iodine needs contact time. Longer wait = more effective. 30 minutes is the minimum.

steps.instruction: Add vitamin C powder after wait time to improve taste.
steps.helpDetail: Vitamin C neutralizes iodine taste. Add AFTER the treatment period, not before. Lemon juice also works.

techniques.name: UV Light Purification (SODIS)
techniques.subtitle: Use sunlight to kill pathogens

steps.instruction: Fill clear PET plastic bottles with clear water.
steps.helpDetail: Water must be clear — pre-filter if turbid. Only works with PET bottles (recycling number 1). Glass and colored plastic block UV.

steps.instruction: Lay bottles on dark surface in direct sunlight for 6 hours.
steps.helpDetail: On a dark rock, corrugated metal roof, or any heat-absorbing surface. Lay horizontally for maximum UV exposure.

steps.instruction: In cloudy weather, extend to 2 full days.
steps.helpDetail: UV-A radiation kills bacteria and viruses. Combined with heat, it's highly effective. Does NOT remove chemical contaminants.

techniques.name: Improvised Sock Filter
techniques.subtitle: Remove sediment from turbid water

steps.instruction: Layer materials inside a sock or pant leg: charcoal, sand, gravel.
steps.helpDetail: Bottom (outlet): fine sand or crushed charcoal. Middle: sand. Top: gravel and grass. Each layer should be 2-3 inches.

steps.instruction: Pour water through slowly and collect from the bottom.
steps.helpDetail: First few runs will be cloudy. Discard the first 2-3 batches. The filter improves with use as it develops a biofilm.

steps.instruction: STILL PURIFY the filtered water by boiling or chemical treatment.
steps.helpDetail: This filter removes sediment and some bacteria but does NOT guarantee safe water. It's a pre-treatment step.

techniques.name: Charcoal Water Filter
techniques.subtitle: Activated charcoal removes chemicals and taste

steps.instruction: Make charcoal from hardwood burned down to coals.
steps.helpDetail: Let a hot fire burn to glowing coals, then smother with dirt. Once cool, crush into small pieces. This is NOT activated carbon but still useful.

steps.instruction: Crush charcoal into fine granules.
steps.helpDetail: The finer the better — more surface area means better filtration. Remove any large chunks.

steps.instruction: Build a filter: container with hole → grass → charcoal → sand → gravel.
steps.helpDetail: Use a cut bottle, bark funnel, or bamboo section. The tight-packed charcoal layer should be the thickest — at least 4 inches.

steps.instruction: Run water through multiple times for clearer results.
steps.helpDetail: Each pass improves clarity. Even so, boil or chemically treat afterward. Charcoal improves taste significantly.

techniques.name: Solar Still
techniques.subtitle: Extract water from the ground using sun

steps.instruction: Dig a hole 3 feet wide and 2 feet deep.
steps.helpDetail: Choose a sunny spot with moist soil. Near vegetation is better — plant roots indicate moisture.

steps.instruction: Place a collection container in the center of the hole.
steps.helpDetail: A cup, can, water bottle — anything that can collect drips. If you have a drinking tube, run it from the container up out of the hole.

steps.instruction: Cover the hole with clear plastic sheeting.
steps.helpDetail: Seal edges with dirt or rocks. Make it airtight. A garbage bag or poncho works.

steps.instruction: Place a small rock on the plastic directly above the container.
steps.helpDetail: This creates a cone shape. Evaporated moisture condenses on the plastic and drips into the container. Expect 1-2 cups per day.

techniques.name: Transpiration Bag
techniques.subtitle: Collect water from living plants

steps.instruction: Place a clear plastic bag over a leafy tree branch.
steps.helpDetail: Choose a branch with lots of green leaves in direct sunlight. Non-poisonous trees only — avoid oleander, yew, rhododendron.

steps.instruction: Tie the bag opening tightly around the branch.
steps.helpDetail: Make it airtight. Weight the bottom corner so water collects there.

steps.instruction: Collect water from the lowest corner after several hours.
steps.helpDetail: A single bag can produce 1-3 cups per day. Set up multiple bags. Move to new branches daily as leaves deplete.

techniques.name: Dew Collection
techniques.subtitle: Gather morning moisture from grass

steps.instruction: Wrap absorbent cloth around your ankles and walk through grass at dawn.
steps.helpDetail: T-shirts, bandanas, or any absorbent fabric. Walk through tall grass, meadows, or low brush.

steps.instruction: Wring the cloth into a container when saturated.
steps.helpDetail: Repeat multiple passes. In heavy dew conditions, you can collect over a liter per hour.

steps.instruction: Purify if possible — dew from grass may contain contaminants.
steps.helpDetail: Dew is condensed atmospheric moisture and is usually clean, but plant surfaces may harbor bacteria.

techniques.name: Rain Collection
techniques.subtitle: The cleanest natural water source

steps.instruction: Spread any waterproof material to maximize collection area.
steps.helpDetail: Tarps, ponchos, garbage bags, large leaves. Angle toward a collection point.

steps.instruction: Use any available containers — bottles, pots, carved wood, pits lined with plastic.
steps.helpDetail: Set out every container you have. A 1-hour moderate rain on a 6x6 tarp can yield 5+ gallons.

steps.instruction: Let the first few minutes of rain wash the collection surface.
steps.helpDetail: Initial runoff picks up dirt and contaminants from the collection surface. Discard the first accumulation.

techniques.name: Finding Water by Vegetation
techniques.subtitle: Plants tell you where water is

steps.instruction: Look for green vegetation in dry terrain.
steps.helpDetail: Cottonwoods, willows, sycamores, and cattails indicate water at or near the surface.

steps.instruction: Follow animal trails that converge — they lead to water.
steps.helpDetail: Trails from multiple directions meeting indicate a water source. Follow them downhill.

steps.instruction: Watch birds at dawn and dusk — grain-eating birds fly toward water.
steps.helpDetail: Pigeons, doves, and finches need daily water. They fly in straight lines toward sources. Follow their flight path.

steps.instruction: Dig in the outside bend of dry riverbeds.
steps.helpDetail: Water pools on the outside of bends. Dig 1-3 feet and wait. Seepage indicates groundwater.

techniques.name: Desalinating Seawater
techniques.subtitle: Remove salt through evaporation

steps.instruction: NEVER drink seawater directly — it accelerates dehydration.
steps.helpDetail: Salt concentration in seawater requires your kidneys to use more water than the drink provides. Drinking seawater kills.

steps.instruction: Build a solar still using seawater as the source.
steps.helpDetail: Dig a pit, pour seawater in, cover with plastic. Evaporated water is salt-free. The salt remains in the pit.

steps.instruction: Or boil seawater and collect condensation.
steps.helpDetail: Boil in a covered pot with a tube running from the lid to a collection container. The steam condenses as fresh water.

steps.instruction: Even small amounts of fresh water are worth the effort.
steps.helpDetail: A solar still may only give 1-2 cups per day but that's enough to survive. Set up multiple stills.

techniques.name: Melting Snow & Ice for Water
techniques.subtitle: Never eat snow directly

steps.instruction: Never eat snow directly — it lowers core body temp.
steps.helpDetail: The energy cost of melting snow in your mouth is significant. In a cold environment, this accelerates hypothermia.

steps.instruction: Melt snow or ice in a dark container in the sun.
steps.helpDetail: Black or dark containers absorb heat. Pack loosely — compressed snow takes longer to melt and can scorch the container.

steps.instruction: Prefer ice over snow — ice yields more water per volume.
steps.helpDetail: Snow is 90% air. You need roughly 10 gallons of snow to get 1 gallon of water. Old sea ice (blue/clear) is desalinated.

steps.instruction: Use body heat or ambient sunlight to melt snow.
steps.helpDetail: Place a dark container in direct sunlight or between outer clothing layers. Never put snow directly against your skin to melt—it steals critical core heat.

techniques.name: Water Storage & Transport
techniques.subtitle: Carry more, lose less

steps.instruction: Improvise containers: bamboo sections, animal stomachs, bark vessels.
steps.helpDetail: Green bamboo sections sealed with mud hold water well. Birch bark can be folded into watertight containers.

steps.instruction: Line pits with plastic, large leaves, or clay for ground storage.
steps.helpDetail: A tarp-lined hole can store 10+ gallons. Cover to prevent evaporation and contamination.

steps.instruction: Keep stored water covered and cool.
steps.helpDetail: Bacteria multiply rapidly in warm exposed water. Store in shade and cover to keep insects out.

steps.instruction: Seal containers to prevent spilling during travel.
steps.helpDetail: Use cork, carved wood plugs, or bunched cloth to seal improvised containers.

techniques.name: Water Rationing Strategy
techniques.subtitle: Ration sweat, not water

steps.instruction: Drink when thirsty. Do NOT ration your water.
steps.helpDetail: Your body needs water now, not later. Rationing leads to inefficiency — you become too weak to get more water. Many people have died with water in their canteen.

steps.instruction: Ration your SWEAT instead.
steps.helpDetail: Stay in shade. Move at night. Minimize exertion. Breathe through your nose. Keep covered with loose clothing.

steps.instruction: Know your needs: minimum 1 quart/day resting in shade, 1 gallon/day with activity.
steps.helpDetail: In hot desert conditions, you can lose 1-2 quarts per HOUR of heavy exertion. Monitor urine color.

techniques.name: Condensation Trap
techniques.subtitle: Collect moisture from air using temperature difference

steps.instruction: Find a metal surface—car hood, sheet metal, or large flat rock.
steps.helpDetail: Metal cools faster than air at night, causing condensation. Bigger surface = more water.

steps.instruction: Angle the surface so condensation runs to a collection point.
steps.helpDetail: Even slight angle causes droplets to run downhill. Place container at low end.

steps.instruction: Best results at dawn when temperature difference is greatest.
steps.helpDetail: Desert nights are ideal due to extreme temperature swings. Can yield 1-2 cups overnight.

techniques.name: Bamboo Water Collection
techniques.subtitle: Green bamboo stores water naturally

steps.instruction: Find green bamboo with thick stems. Shake—hear water inside.
steps.helpDetail: Old dry bamboo is empty. Look for green, thick-stemmed living bamboo.

steps.instruction: Bend a tall stalk and cut the top. Water drains out.
steps.helpDetail: Position a container underneath. Each stem segment holds water independently.

steps.instruction: Alternatively, punch a hole just above each node.
steps.helpDetail: Punch, drain, move to next node. Each compartment is separate.

techniques.name: Birch Tree Tapping
techniques.subtitle: Birch sap is drinkable water with nutrients

steps.instruction: Find a birch tree with trunk at least 10 inches diameter.
steps.helpDetail: Works in spring when sap flows. Birch, maple, walnut all produce drinkable sap.

steps.instruction: Carve a small hole 2 inches deep, angled slightly upward.
steps.helpDetail: About 2-3 feet from ground. Use knife point or sharp stick.

steps.instruction: Insert a tube or hollow stick into the hole.
steps.helpDetail: Hollowed elderberry branch works. Directs sap into container.

steps.instruction: Collect sap. Drinkable immediately—slightly sweet.
steps.helpDetail: A large birch produces 1-2 gallons per day in spring. Contains water, sugars, minerals.

techniques.name: Rock Seepage Collection
techniques.subtitle: Collect water seeping from rock faces

steps.instruction: Look for wet rock faces—dark stains, moss, fern growth.
steps.helpDetail: Limestone and sandstone are porous. Water seeps through naturally.

steps.instruction: Press absorbent cloth against wet rock, wring into container.
steps.helpDetail: Bandana works. Slow but reliable. Leave container overnight.

steps.instruction: Create channel with clay or mud to direct trickle flow.
steps.helpDetail: Even small trickles add up over time. Leave collection overnight.

techniques.name: Solar Desalination
techniques.subtitle: Convert seawater to drinkable water using sunlight

steps.instruction: Fill a dark container with seawater. Place a smaller empty container inside.
steps.helpDetail: The outer container holds saltwater. The inner container catches condensation. The inner container must be shorter than the outer.

steps.instruction: Cover with clear plastic wrap. Seal edges tightly.
steps.helpDetail: The cover must be airtight. Use tape, clay, or wet sand to seal edges. Any leak lets moisture escape.

steps.instruction: Place a small weight on the plastic above the inner container.
steps.helpDetail: This creates a low point where condensation drips into the collection container. A small stone works perfectly.

steps.instruction: Place in direct sunlight. Collect fresh water from inner container.
steps.helpDetail: Sun heats saltwater, evaporation condenses on plastic, drips into inner container. Yield: 500ml-1L per day in strong sun.

techniques.name: Birch Bark Water Container
techniques.subtitle: Waterproof container from tree bark

steps.instruction: Harvest a large sheet of birch bark without damaging the tree.
steps.helpDetail: Cut horizontally around the trunk, then vertically to peel. Take bark from dead or fallen trees when possible to avoid harming living trees.

steps.instruction: Fold into a box or cone shape. Secure with cordage or wooden pegs.
steps.helpDetail: Birch bark is naturally waterproof due to betulin compounds. Fold with the white side OUT. Corners can be folded and pinned.

steps.instruction: Seal seams with heated pine resin.
steps.helpDetail: Mix pine resin with charcoal dust for a strong waterproof sealant. Apply warm, it hardens as it cools.

steps.instruction: You can even boil water in birch bark directly over fire.
steps.helpDetail: Birch bark containers can go directly over flame. The water inside prevents the bark from burning. The bark ignites at 450°F but water keeps it below 212°F.

techniques.name: Water Vine Extraction
techniques.subtitle: Cut tropical vines for immediate drinking water

steps.instruction: Identify thick, woody vines in tropical or subtropical forest.
steps.helpDetail: Water vines are 1-3 inches thick with rough bark. Common species: grape vine, rattan, and various lianas. Avoid milky or colored sap — these are toxic.

steps.instruction: Cut the vine HIGH first, then cut low.
steps.helpDetail: Critical: cut the top first to break the capillary seal. If you cut the bottom first, water retreats upward and you get nothing.

steps.instruction: Hold the cut section over your mouth. Water drips from the bottom cut.
steps.helpDetail: Clear, tasteless water is safe. If the liquid is colored, milky, or bitter, spit it out immediately — that vine is not a water source.

techniques.name: Fog Collection
techniques.subtitle: Harvest water from fog and mist

steps.instruction: Stretch fabric or mesh between two poles at a 90° angle to prevailing wind.
steps.helpDetail: The mesh catches fog droplets which coalesce and run down. Position on a ridge or exposed hillside where fog flows across.

steps.instruction: Channel the bottom edge into a collection container.
steps.helpDetail: Create a V-shaped trough at the bottom of the mesh using bark, plastic, or folded fabric to funnel water into your container.

steps.instruction: Leave overnight. Fog is heaviest from midnight to dawn.
steps.helpDetail: In coastal areas with regular fog, yields of 1-5 liters per square meter per night are common. This is used as primary water supply in some coastal deserts.

techniques.name: Digging a Water Well
techniques.subtitle: Access groundwater in low-lying areas

steps.instruction: Choose a location: outside bends of dry riverbeds, valley bottoms, base of hills.
steps.helpDetail: Water collects at the lowest accessible point. The outside bend of a river channel has deeper water table. Green vegetation in dry terrain marks subsurface water.

steps.instruction: Dig a hole 1-3 feet deep. Widen as you go for stability.
steps.helpDetail: Start with a small hole. If soil becomes damp, you're close. If dry after 3 feet, try a different location.

steps.instruction: Wait 30 minutes for water to seep in from surrounding soil.
steps.helpDetail: The first water will be muddy. Let it settle, then scoop the clearest water from the top into your container.

steps.instruction: Filter and purify before drinking — groundwater still needs treatment.
steps.helpDetail: Groundwater is usually cleaner than surface water but can contain bacteria and parasites. Always boil or chemically treat.

techniques.name: Snow Melting Best Practices
techniques.subtitle: Convert snow to water without losing body heat

steps.instruction: Never eat snow directly — it lowers core body temperature dangerously.
steps.helpDetail: Eating snow uses body heat to melt it. In cold conditions, this accelerates hypothermia. It takes 1 calorie to melt 1 gram of snow.

steps.instruction: Melt snow in a dark container in sunlight, or near (not on) fire.
steps.helpDetail: A dark bottle in the sun melts snow with zero energy cost to you. Near a fire is next best. Pack snow tightly for more water per volume.

steps.instruction: Start with a small amount of water in the container, then add snow gradually.
steps.helpDetail: If you put a container of dry snow directly on heat, the bottom burns before the top melts. A water base conducts heat evenly.

steps.instruction: Use ice instead of snow when available — it yields more water per volume.
steps.helpDetail: Snow is 90% air. A liter of snow yields only ~100ml water. Ice yields ~900ml. Always prefer ice to snow.

techniques.name: Water Safety Assessment
techniques.subtitle: Evaluate unknown water sources before drinking

steps.instruction: Check clarity — hold water up to light. Clear is better than cloudy.
steps.helpDetail: Turbidity indicates suspended particles. These particles can harbor bacteria and interfere with chemical treatment. Always filter turbid water first.

steps.instruction: Smell the water — chemical or sulfur odors mean contamination.
steps.helpDetail: Clean water has no smell. Rotten egg smell = hydrogen sulfide. Chemical smell = industrial contamination. Sweet smell = algae blooms (some toxic).

steps.instruction: Look at the surroundings — dead animals, algae blooms, or industrial runoff.
steps.helpDetail: Dead fish or animals near water = toxins. Blue-green algae mats = cyanobacteria (potentially fatal). Upstream mines or farms = chemical contamination.

steps.instruction: When in doubt, purify. The cost of purification is always less than the cost of illness.
steps.helpDetail: Even clear, odorless water from a pristine stream can contain Giardia or Crypto. Always purify unless collecting direct rainfall.

techniques.name: Enhanced Transpiration Bag
techniques.subtitle: Maximize water output from vegetation bags

steps.instruction: Select a sun-facing branch with the most leafy growth.
steps.helpDetail: South-facing branches (Northern Hemisphere) transpire most. More leaves = more water. Avoid toxic plants (oleander, rhododendron).

steps.instruction: Enclose the leafy end in a clear plastic bag. Seal airtight around the branch.
steps.helpDetail: Use string, tape, or twisted plastic to seal. Any air leak wastes moisture. The bag should balloon with trapped air.

steps.instruction: Place a small rock in the bottom corner to create a collection point.
steps.helpDetail: Water condenses on the inside of the bag and runs down to the lowest point. The rock creates a pool you can access.

steps.instruction: Set up multiple bags — each yields only 60-200ml per day.
steps.helpDetail: One bag is not enough. Set 5-10 bags to get meaningful water. Replace branches daily as transpiration declines after cutting.

techniques.name: Cactus Water Extraction
techniques.subtitle: Extract moisture from desert cacti (with caution)

steps.instruction: Identify barrel cactus (Ferocactus) — the only safe species for water.
steps.helpDetail: NOT all cacti contain drinkable water. Many contain toxic alkaloids that cause vomiting (which accelerates dehydration). Barrel cactus is the safest option. Prickly pear fruit is also safe.

steps.instruction: Cut off the top of the cactus and mash the pulp inside.
steps.helpDetail: Use a knife or rock to slice the top. The interior pulp is moist and fibrous. Mash it to release moisture. Do NOT drink the liquid directly — chew the pulp and spit out the fiber.

steps.instruction: Squeeze pulp through cloth to extract moisture. Drink in small amounts.
steps.helpDetail: Yield is small: a large barrel cactus may give 1-2 cups. The liquid may taste bitter. Drink slowly. If it causes nausea, stop — you have the wrong species.

techniques.name: Coconut Water Safety
techniques.subtitle: Use green coconuts for hydration — avoid mature ones

steps.instruction: Select GREEN coconuts (young). Avoid brown mature coconuts.
steps.helpDetail: Green coconut water is sterile, isotonic, and safe to drink in quantity. Brown coconut water is higher in oil and can act as a laxative, causing dehydration if consumed in large amounts.

steps.instruction: Pierce the soft eye of the coconut with a sharp point or knife.
steps.helpDetail: Each coconut has 2-3 'eyes' (dark spots) at one end. One eye is softer than the others — test each with your knife tip. Pierce through this soft eye to access the water inside.

steps.instruction: Drink directly or pour into container. Limit mature coconut to 2-3 per day.
steps.helpDetail: Green coconuts: drink freely. Mature coconuts: limit to 2-3 daily to avoid digestive issues. Coconut water contains electrolytes (potassium, sodium) making it superior to plain water for rehydration.

techniques.name: Banana Plant Water
techniques.subtitle: Extract water from banana plant trunks

steps.instruction: Cut a banana plant trunk down to a 6-inch stump.
steps.helpDetail: Banana 'trunks' are actually tightly rolled leaf sheaths, not wood. They cut easily. Choose a mature plant for maximum water content.

steps.instruction: Scoop out the center of the stump to create a bowl shape.
steps.helpDetail: Hollow out the core of the stump. Within 10-15 minutes, water will seep up from the root system and fill the bowl. The plant acts as a natural pump.

steps.instruction: Scoop water every 30 minutes. The stump refills 3-4 times.
steps.helpDetail: Each filling yields about 1 cup. Scoop out the first fill (may be bitter) and drink subsequent fills. A single plant can provide 2-3 cups of water over several hours.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Clay Pot Filter (Ceramic)
techniques.subtitle: Make a ceramic filter that removes bacteria

steps.instruction: Mix clay with fine sawdust or rice husks (60% clay, 40% organic).
steps.helpDetail: The organic material burns out during firing, leaving microscopic pores in the clay. These pores are small enough to filter bacteria (0.5-2 microns). The ratio is critical.

steps.instruction: Shape into a pot or bowl and let dry completely (1-2 days).
steps.helpDetail: Form a bowl shape with walls about 1 inch thick. It must dry completely before firing or it will crack. Keep in shade and turn periodically for even drying.

steps.instruction: Fire in a hot fire for 6-8 hours. The organic material burns out leaving pores.
steps.helpDetail: Maintain high heat. The sawdust vaporizes, leaving a network of micropores. After firing and cooling, test: water should seep through slowly (1-2 liters per hour). Too fast = pores too large.

techniques.name: Bio-Sand Filter
techniques.subtitle: Build a biological filtration system for long-term use

steps.instruction: Fill a large container with layers: gravel (bottom), coarse sand, fine sand (top).
steps.helpDetail: Use a 5-gallon bucket or similar. Bottom 4 inches: gravel (pea-sized). Middle 4 inches: coarse sand. Top 8 inches: fine sand. Each layer filters progressively smaller particles.

steps.instruction: Create an outlet at the bottom for filtered water to exit.
steps.helpDetail: Drill or punch a hole near the bottom. Insert a tube or bamboo section. Water enters the top, filters through layers, and exits clean at the bottom. Flow rate should be slow: 1 liter per 15-20 minutes.

steps.instruction: Use daily for 2 weeks. A biological layer (schmutzdecke) forms and filters bacteria.
steps.helpDetail: The magic is the biofilm that forms on the top sand layer after 2 weeks of regular use. This living layer consumes pathogens. Once established, the filter removes 96-99% of bacteria. Never let it dry out.

techniques.name: Morning Dew Collection (Cloth Drag)
techniques.subtitle: Collect dew by dragging cloth through wet grass

steps.instruction: Before sunrise, tie absorbent cloth around your ankles and legs.
steps.helpDetail: T-shirts, towels, bandanas — any absorbent cotton material. Wrap around lower legs and feet. Walk through tall, dew-covered grass in the early morning.

steps.instruction: Walk through tall grass, collecting dew on the cloth.
steps.helpDetail: Walk slowly through areas with heavy dew. The cloth absorbs moisture from the grass. Each pass through a 50-foot stretch saturates the cloth significantly.

steps.instruction: Wring the cloth into a container. Repeat until enough water is collected.
steps.helpDetail: Each wringing yields 1-4 ounces depending on dew intensity and cloth size. In heavy dew conditions, you can collect 1+ liters in an hour. The water is generally clean but filter if possible.

techniques.name: Seawater Ice Desalination
techniques.subtitle: Freeze seawater to separate salt from ice

steps.instruction: In freezing conditions, collect seawater in shallow containers.
steps.helpDetail: Shallow containers freeze faster. The physics: when seawater freezes, salt is excluded from the ice crystal structure and concentrates in the remaining liquid brine.

steps.instruction: Let water freeze partially. Pour off the unfrozen brine (concentrated salt).
steps.helpDetail: The liquid remaining is super-salty brine. Pour it off. The ice that formed is significantly less salty than the original seawater. This is the desalinated portion.

steps.instruction: Melt the ice. Taste test — repeat freeze/pour if still salty.
steps.helpDetail: First freeze removes about 70% of salt. A second freeze-pour cycle gets it close to drinkable. Old sea ice (multi-year) found in Arctic regions is naturally fresh — it has a blue tint.

techniques.name: Tarp Rain Catchment
techniques.subtitle: Maximize rain collection with a tarp funnel

steps.instruction: Spread tarp with edges elevated and center dipped to form a funnel.
steps.helpDetail: Tie corners to trees or stakes at waist height. The center should sag to collect water. A 6x8 foot tarp can collect 25+ gallons in 1 inch of rain.

steps.instruction: Place a container under the lowest point of the tarp.
steps.helpDetail: Poke a small hole or create a channel at the lowest point. Water drains down the funnel shape and into your container. Angle the tarp slightly for continuous drainage.

steps.instruction: Pre-rinse the tarp if dirty. First flush may contain debris.
steps.helpDetail: Let the first 30 seconds of rain wash dirt off the tarp before collecting. Rain water collected on a clean surface is safe to drink without treatment in most environments.

techniques.name: Activated Charcoal Making
techniques.subtitle: Create filtering charcoal for water purification

steps.instruction: Make regular charcoal by burning hardwood in a low-oxygen fire.
steps.helpDetail: Burn hardwood in a covered pit or sealed container with a vent hole. The wood converts to charcoal (carbon) without burning to ash. Cool completely before handling.

steps.instruction: Crush charcoal into small granules (pea-sized to sand-sized).
steps.helpDetail: Smaller pieces have more surface area for adsorption. Crush with rocks between two flat surfaces. Remove any unburned wood — it should be uniformly black and lightweight.

steps.instruction: Layer crushed charcoal in filter between sand layers.
steps.helpDetail: Charcoal adsorbs chemical contaminants, chlorine, and some organic toxins. It does NOT reliably kill bacteria or viruses — still boil or treat chemically after charcoal filtering. Replace charcoal weekly.

techniques.name: Spring Identification
techniques.subtitle: Find natural spring water sources

steps.instruction: Look for unusually green vegetation on hillsides in dry areas.
steps.helpDetail: Springs create strips or patches of green plants in otherwise dry terrain. The vegetation line often follows the underground water flow path. Willows, cattails, and reeds almost always indicate nearby water.

steps.instruction: Check the base of cliffs, rock outcrops, and where geology changes.
steps.helpDetail: Springs form where permeable rock (sandstone, limestone) meets impermeable rock (clay, granite). Water travels through porous rock until it hits a barrier and seeps out. These geological boundaries are spring sites.

steps.instruction: Test: spring water is typically cold, clear, and flowing.
steps.helpDetail: Good springs produce clear, cold water (ground temperature, ~55°F). If the water is warm, cloudy, or has an odor, it may be surface runoff, not a true spring. True spring water is generally safe but still filter if possible.

techniques.name: Underground Seep Location
techniques.subtitle: Find subsurface water by reading terrain clues

steps.instruction: Dig at the outside bend of a dry streambed.
steps.helpDetail: Water flows underground in the same path as the surface stream. The outside of a bend has the deepest water table. Dig 1-3 feet deep. Water seeps in from the sides and bottom.

steps.instruction: Dig at the base of a sand dune facing the valley.
steps.helpDetail: Dunes collect and concentrate rainwater. The valley-facing (concave) side often has water closer to the surface. Dig where the dune meets the valley floor.

steps.instruction: Wait for the hole to fill. Filter and purify the seep water.
steps.helpDetail: Seep water may take 15-60 minutes to collect. Let sediment settle before scooping. The water has been naturally filtered through soil but may still contain pathogens — purify before drinking.

techniques.name: Altitude Boiling Adjustments
techniques.subtitle: Understand how altitude affects water purification

steps.instruction: At sea level, water boils at 212°F (100°C). At altitude, it boils at lower temperatures.
steps.helpDetail: For every 1,000 feet of elevation, the boiling point drops about 2°F. At 10,000 feet, water boils at 193°F. At 14,000 feet: 187°F. This matters for purification.

steps.instruction: Good news: pathogens die well below boiling temperature.
steps.helpDetail: Most waterborne pathogens die at 160°F (71°C). Even at extreme altitude (Everest base camp at 17,600 feet), water boils at 179°F — still hot enough to kill all common pathogens.

steps.instruction: Bring water to a rolling boil for 1 minute (3 minutes above 6,500 feet).
steps.helpDetail: CDC recommendation. At sea level, 1 minute is sufficient. Above 6,500 feet, boil for 3 minutes to compensate for the lower temperature. Rolling boil means large, vigorous bubbles.

techniques.name: Animal Indicators for Water
techniques.subtitle: Follow animal behavior to find water sources

steps.instruction: Follow grazing animals (deer, cattle) at dawn and dusk — they head to water.
steps.helpDetail: Grazing animals drink at dawn and dusk. Their trails converge toward water sources. Fresh tracks in dry areas always lead to water if you follow them long enough.

steps.instruction: Watch birds: grain-eaters fly toward water at dusk, away at dawn.
steps.helpDetail: Seed-eating birds (finches, pigeons, doves) must drink daily. In the evening, they fly fast and low toward water. In the morning, they fly away from water. Follow their evening flight path.

steps.instruction: Listen for frogs and insects at night — they indicate water nearby.
steps.helpDetail: Frog calls mean standing water within earshot. Dense insect swarms (mosquitoes, midges) often hover over or near water sources. Bees are rarely more than 3 miles from water.

techniques.name: Water from Wild Fruits & Plants
techniques.subtitle: Extract moisture from edible plants and fruits

steps.instruction: Identify water-rich wild fruits: berries, wild grapes, prickly pear.
steps.helpDetail: Many wild fruits are 80-90% water. Prickly pear pads and fruit, wild grapes, blackberries, blueberries, and even dandelion stems contain usable moisture.

steps.instruction: Eat water-rich plants: cattail shoots, wild lettuce, clover.
steps.helpDetail: Young cattail shoots taste like cucumber and are mostly water. Wild lettuce, chickweed, and sorrel are high in moisture. Only eat plants you can positively identify.

steps.instruction: Chew but do not swallow fibrous plants to extract moisture.
steps.helpDetail: Green grass, sugar cane, and young bark can be chewed to extract moisture. Chew thoroughly, swallow the juice, spit out the fiber. This provides small but meaningful amounts of hydration.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Emergency Urban Water Sources
techniques.subtitle: Find drinkable water in buildings and vehicles after disaster

steps.instruction: Hot water heater: 30-80 gallons of safe, stored water.
steps.helpDetail: Turn off power/gas to heater. Open the drain valve at the bottom into a container. The water is pre-heated and essentially sterile. One heater can sustain a person for weeks.

steps.instruction: Toilet tanks (NOT bowls): contain 1-2 gallons of clean water.
steps.helpDetail: The TANK (upper portion) holds clean water, same as tap water. Do NOT use water from the bowl. Do NOT use if chemical cleaning tablets are in the tank (blue/green water).

steps.instruction: Pipe drainage: open the lowest faucet to drain remaining water from pipes.
steps.helpDetail: After water supply stops, pipes still contain residual water. Open the lowest faucet in the building. Gravity drains water from upper pipes down. May yield 1-5 gallons.

techniques.name: Poncho Rain Collection
techniques.subtitle: Use a poncho or garbage bag as rain catchment

steps.instruction: Tie corners of poncho to trees or stakes at waist height.
steps.helpDetail: Create a basin shape with the center lower than the edges. A garbage bag can be cut open to create a flat sheet. Even a jacket opened and stretched works for small amounts.

steps.instruction: Place a small rock in the center to create a funnel point.
steps.helpDetail: The rock weighs down the center, creating a natural funnel. All water flows toward this low point. Punch or cut a small hole and place your container below for automatic collection.

steps.instruction: In light rain, maximize catchment area. Move to open ground.
steps.helpDetail: Tree canopy intercepts significant rainfall. Move catchment to the most open area available. A 3x3 foot poncho in moderate rain collects about 1 gallon per hour.

techniques.name: Improvised Oral Rehydration Solution
techniques.subtitle: Make WHO-standard rehydration fluid from basic ingredients

steps.instruction: Mix: 1 liter clean water + 6 teaspoons sugar + ½ teaspoon salt.
steps.helpDetail: This is the WHO oral rehydration formula. The precise ratio matters — too much sugar causes diarrhea; too much salt causes vomiting. Measure carefully.

steps.instruction: Stir thoroughly until fully dissolved. Taste should be like tears.
steps.helpDetail: The solution should taste mildly salty, similar to tears or slightly salted water. If it tastes very salty, add more water. If too sweet, add a pinch more salt.

steps.instruction: Sip slowly over 4-6 hours. Do not gulp.
steps.helpDetail: Rapid drinking can trigger vomiting in dehydrated individuals. Small, frequent sips allow maximum absorption. This solution treats dehydration 10x more effectively than plain water because of electrolyte replacement.

techniques.name: Tree Sap as Water Source
techniques.subtitle: Tap trees for clean, drinkable sap

steps.instruction: In late winter/spring, many trees produce copious drinkable sap: maple, birch, walnut, sycamore.
steps.helpDetail: Sap is 97-98% pure water with dissolved sugars and minerals. It requires no purification. Flow is strongest during freeze-thaw cycles.

steps.instruction: Cut a V-shaped notch into the bark on the south-facing side, 3 feet from the ground.
steps.helpDetail: Cut through bark into the sapwood. The V-shape channels sap downward to a drip point. Do not cut too deep or you damage the tree.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cut a V-shaped notch into the

steps.instruction: Insert a spout: hollowed elder branch, folded bark channel, or angled stick under the cut.
steps.helpDetail: The spout directs sap flow into your collection container. A sloped surface catches sap more efficiently than a vertical wound.

steps.instruction: Collect 1-3 liters per day per tap depending on tree size and conditions.
steps.helpDetail: Production peaks when nights freeze and days are above 40°F. Large trees can yield more. Multiple taps on one tree are fine.

steps.instruction: Drink sap raw — no purification needed. It tastes mildly sweet and clean.
steps.helpDetail: Sap is produced behind the bark in a sterile environment. Fresh sap is safe to drink directly. It ferments and spoils within 2-3 days in warm weather.

techniques.name: Beach Well
techniques.subtitle: Find fresh water at the ocean beach

steps.instruction: Dig above the high-tide line in a low area between sand dunes.
steps.helpDetail: Fresh water floats on top of salt water underground. Above the tide line, a freshwater lens exists that can be tapped by digging.
steps.illustrationDescription: Dig above the high-tide line i

steps.instruction: Dig until water begins seeping into the hole — typically 2-4 feet deep.
steps.helpDetail: Stop digging and let the hole fill naturally. The first water may be slightly brackish. Let it clear for 30 minutes.

steps.instruction: Taste the water. If only slightly salty, it is drinkable in an emergency.
steps.helpDetail: Truly fresh water from beach wells tastes clean. Brackish water (slightly salty) is still better than no water. Very salty water should be avoided.
steps.illustrationDescription: Taste the water. If only sligh

steps.instruction: Line the well walls with rocks or sticks to prevent sand from collapsing into the water.
steps.helpDetail: A lined well produces cleaner water and lasts longer. Pack rocks tightly to filter sand. Add a bark cover to prevent contamination.
steps.illustrationDescription: Line the well walls with rocks

steps.instruction: If water is too salty, dig farther from the ocean or at higher elevation behind the first dunes.
steps.helpDetail: The fresh water lens gets thicker farther from the ocean. Vegetation presence indicates fresh water availability — dig where plants grow.
steps.illustrationDescription: If water is too salty, dig far

techniques.name: Cloth Filtration Layers
techniques.subtitle: Filter water through layered fabric for clarity

steps.instruction: Fold cloth into 4-8 layers. T-shirt cotton, bandana, or sock material all work.
steps.helpDetail: More layers = better filtration. 8 layers of cotton fabric remove >99% of sediment and many larger parasites.

steps.instruction: Pour turbid water slowly through the cloth into a clean container.
steps.helpDetail: Let gravity do the work. Don't squeeze — squeezing forces particles through. Slow dripping produces cleaner results.

steps.instruction: For better results, create a layered filter: cloth > sand > charcoal > cloth > gravel.
steps.helpDetail: Each layer removes different contaminants. Cloth catches large particles, sand catches fine sediment, charcoal adsorbs chemicals.
steps.illustrationDescription: For better results, create a l

steps.instruction: This removes sediment, large parasites, and some bacteria — but NOT viruses or dissolved chemicals.
steps.helpDetail: Cloth filtration is a PRE-TREATMENT. You still need to boil or chemically treat the filtered water to kill pathogens.

steps.instruction: Replace cloth filters when they become clogged. Rinse and dry in sun to partially sterilize.
steps.helpDetail: UV from sunlight kills bacteria on the cloth surface. A clean filter works much better than a dirty one. Carry spare cloth.

techniques.name: Ceramic Filter Construction
techniques.subtitle: Build a gravity-fed ceramic water filter

steps.instruction: Mix clay soil with fine sawdust, rice hulls, or coffee grounds in a 60:40 ratio.
steps.helpDetail: The organic material creates micro-pores when the clay is fired. These pores are small enough to filter bacteria but allow water to pass.
steps.illustrationDescription: Mix clay soil with fine sawdus

steps.instruction: Form the mixture into a pot or bowl shape, 8-10 inches diameter, 1/2 inch thick walls.
steps.helpDetail: The shape must hold water. A concave pot that nests inside a larger container works best for gravity-feed filtering.
steps.illustrationDescription: Form the mixture into a pot or

steps.instruction: Dry the formed pot completely — at least 24-48 hours in warm air.
steps.helpDetail: Rapid drying causes cracking. Dry slowly in shade for the first day, then in sun. The pot should be bone-dry before firing.

steps.instruction: Fire the pot in a bonfire for 6-8 hours at sustained high temperature.
steps.helpDetail: The heat burns away the organic material (sawdust/hulls), leaving microscopic pores. Higher temperature = harder ceramic = better filter.
steps.illustrationDescription: Fire the pot in a bonfire for

steps.instruction: Test: fill with muddy water. Clean water should slowly drip through the bottom. Flow rate: 1-2 liters per hour.
steps.helpDetail: A working ceramic filter removes 99%+ of bacteria and all parasites. Coat with colloidal silver (if available) to also kill viruses. Filter lasts for years.
steps.illustrationDescription: Test: fill with muddy water. C

techniques.name: UV Sun Purification (SODIS)
techniques.subtitle: Sterilize water using ultraviolet sunlight

steps.instruction: Fill a clear plastic or glass bottle with water. Pre-filter if turbid — water must be clear.
steps.helpDetail: UV light cannot penetrate murky water. Filter through cloth first if needed. Water should look clear when held up to the light.
steps.illustrationDescription: Fill a clear plastic or glass

steps.instruction: Lay the bottle on a reflective surface (metal sheet, foil, light-colored rock) in full sun.
steps.helpDetail: The reflective surface bounces UV through the water from below, doubling the UV dose. Dark surfaces absorb instead of reflecting.
steps.illustrationDescription: Lay the bottle on a reflective

steps.instruction: Leave in direct sunlight for minimum 6 hours. If partly cloudy, extend to 2 full days.
steps.helpDetail: UV-A radiation damages pathogen DNA, rendering them unable to reproduce. Full sun for 6 hours achieves 99.9% pathogen reduction.
steps.illustrationDescription: Leave in direct sunlight for m

steps.instruction: Shake the bottle every hour to oxygenate — the combination of UV + oxygen is more lethal to pathogens.
steps.helpDetail: Dissolved oxygen creates reactive oxygen species under UV light. This secondary mechanism kills pathogens that UV alone might miss.
steps.illustrationDescription: Shake the bottle every hour to

steps.instruction: Water is safe to drink after the treatment period. This method kills bacteria, viruses, and protozoa.
steps.helpDetail: SODIS is WHO-endorsed and used worldwide. It does NOT remove chemical contamination. Combine with filtration for best results.
steps.illustrationDescription: Water is safe to drink after t

techniques.name: Dew Trap (Advanced)
techniques.subtitle: Maximize dew collection with engineered surfaces

steps.instruction: Spread a large plastic sheet, tarp, or emergency blanket on the ground in the evening.
steps.helpDetail: Position in an open area away from trees. The material cools faster than surrounding ground, causing moisture to condense on its surface.

steps.instruction: Create a slight bowl shape by raising the edges with rocks or sticks.
steps.helpDetail: The concave shape channels condensed dew toward the center low point where you can collect it.
steps.illustrationDescription: Create a slight bowl shape by

steps.instruction: Place a clean container at the lowest point in the center.
steps.helpDetail: A cup, can, or bark container catches the dew as it runs to the center. Weight the container so it doesn't tip.
steps.illustrationDescription: Place a clean container at the

steps.instruction: In addition: wrap cloth around your ankles and walk through dewy grass at dawn. Wring cloth into a container.
steps.helpDetail: Grass holds enormous amounts of dew. A 30-minute walk through knee-high dewy grass can yield 500mL+ of clean water.

steps.instruction: Best conditions for dew: clear nights, calm wind, temperature difference between air and surface.
steps.helpDetail: Desert environments produce the most dew despite low humidity — the large day-night temperature swing causes heavy condensation.

techniques.name: Seep Well Improvement
techniques.subtitle: Enhance a natural water seep into a usable spring

steps.instruction: Find a natural seep: muddy ground on a hillside, moss growth in dry terrain, insect swarms over wet soil.
steps.helpDetail: Seeps indicate groundwater reaching the surface. Even a trickle can be developed into a significant water source with effort.
steps.illustrationDescription: Find a natural seep: muddy gro

steps.instruction: Dig a basin at the seep point — 12 inches deep, 18 inches wide.
steps.helpDetail: Excavating gives the water room to pool. Line with rocks to prevent mud from clouding the water. Let it settle for 1 hour.
steps.illustrationDescription: Dig a basin at the seep point

steps.instruction: Create an overflow channel on one side to prevent the basin from flooding and becoming muddy.
steps.helpDetail: Controlled overflow keeps the pool clear and prevents mud from washing back in. Direct overflow away from your camp.

steps.instruction: Line the approach path with rocks to prevent foot traffic from muddying the water source.
steps.helpDetail: Designate one approach path. Lay flat rocks as stepping stones. Always collect water from the uphill side of the pool.
steps.illustrationDescription: Line the approach path with ro

steps.instruction: Cover the basin with bark or a flat rock when not in use to prevent contamination from animals and debris.
steps.helpDetail: A covered seep stays cleaner and collects more water. The cover also reduces evaporation in hot weather.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cover the basin with bark or a

techniques.name: Water Rationing Schedule
techniques.subtitle: Optimize water consumption in scarcity

steps.instruction: NEVER ration water below 500mL (1 pint) per day — your body needs minimum water to function.
steps.helpDetail: Below this level, organs begin failing. It's better to drink what you have now and search for more than to ration so severely you can't function.

steps.instruction: Priority 1: sip small amounts frequently rather than gulping large amounts at once.
steps.helpDetail: Small, frequent sips (1-2 oz every 30 minutes) maximize absorption. Large drinks trigger urination, wasting some water.

steps.instruction: Priority 2: minimize water loss — rest in shade during heat, breathe through your nose, avoid sweating.
steps.helpDetail: You lose 1+ liter per hour through sweating during exertion in heat. Working at dawn/dusk and resting midday saves substantial water.

steps.instruction: Priority 3: eat less if water is scarce — digestion requires water.
steps.helpDetail: Protein digestion requires the most water. In water-scarce situations, reduce food intake, especially protein. Carbohydrates and fats require less water to digest.

steps.instruction: Signs of dehydration: dark urine, headache, dizziness, rapid heartbeat. If these appear, increase intake immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Clear to light yellow urine = adequate hydration. Dark yellow or amber = dehydrating. No urination for 12+ hours = severe dehydration requiring urgent water.

techniques.name: Rock Catchment Basin
techniques.subtitle: Collect rainwater using natural rock features

steps.instruction: Find natural depressions in rock surfaces — these collect and hold rainwater.
steps.helpDetail: Granite outcrops, sandstone ledges, and lava rock all form natural basins. After rain, check every rock depression in your area.

steps.instruction: Improve natural basins: clear debris, deepen with rock tools, create channels to direct more runoff.
steps.helpDetail: Chip away at the basin edges to expand capacity. Carve small channels in the rock that lead water from surrounding surface area into the basin.

steps.instruction: Cover the basin with flat rocks or bark when not collecting to reduce evaporation.
steps.helpDetail: Open rock basins lose water quickly to evaporation and animal consumption. A cover reduces loss by 80%.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cover the basin with flat rock

steps.instruction: Build artificial basins by stacking rocks with clay mortar in a natural drainage path.
steps.helpDetail: Clay-sealed rock walls create a dam that captures runoff. Even a small dam in a gully captures significant water during rain.

steps.instruction: Treat collected rainwater as potentially contaminated — bird droppings and debris can pollute standing rock pools.
steps.helpDetail: Rainwater itself is clean, but surface contamination is common. Filter and purify before drinking, especially if water has been standing.

techniques.name: Field Water Testing
techniques.subtitle: Assess water safety without lab equipment

steps.instruction: Visual test: clear water is NOT necessarily safe, but cloudy, colored, or oily water is definitely suspect.
steps.helpDetail: Pathogens are invisible. Clear water may contain bacteria, viruses, and parasites. However, visible contamination always indicates unsafe water.
steps.illustrationDescription: Visual test: clear water is NO

steps.instruction: Smell test: if water smells of chemicals, sulfur (rotten eggs), or sewage — do not drink. Odorless is better but not proof of safety.
steps.helpDetail: Chemical smells indicate industrial or agricultural contamination that boiling CANNOT remove. Sulfur smell may indicate geothermal contamination.
steps.illustrationDescription: Smell test: if water smells of

steps.instruction: Flow test: flowing water is generally safer than stagnant water. Spring sources are safest natural water.
steps.helpDetail: Moving water contains more oxygen and supports fewer pathogens. Stagnant pools breed mosquito larvae and algal toxins.
steps.illustrationDescription: Flow test: flowing water is ge

steps.instruction: Animal indicators: animals drinking from a source is a PARTIALLY positive sign, but animals tolerate pathogens humans cannot.
steps.helpDetail: Deer and birds drinking from water does NOT mean it's safe for humans. However, absence of any animal life near water is a red flag.

steps.instruction: When in doubt: ALWAYS purify. Boiling (1 minute rolling boil) kills all biological pathogens.
steps.helpDetail: No field test replaces purification. Assume ALL natural water sources are contaminated. The consequences of untreated water (dysentery, giardia) can be fatal in survival situations.

techniques.name: Maple & Birch Tapping
techniques.subtitle: Full tapping technique for sap water

steps.instruction: Select a tree at least 10 inches diameter. Maple and birch are best — both produce abundant, drinkable sap.
steps.helpDetail: Smaller trees cannot sustain tapping without damage. One tap per 10 inches of diameter. A 20-inch tree can handle two taps.
steps.illustrationDescription: Select a tree at least 10 inch

steps.instruction: Using a knife, drill, or sharpened stick, create a hole 2 inches deep, angled slightly upward.
steps.helpDetail: The upward angle ensures sap flows outward via gravity. Position the hole on the south side of the tree for maximum sun exposure.
steps.illustrationDescription: Using a knife, drill, or sharp

steps.instruction: Carve a spile from elderberry, sumac, or any wood with soft pith — hollow out or split for channel.
steps.helpDetail: The spile should fit snugly in the hole. A 4-5 inch tube with a hollow center. Tap gently into the hole with a rock.

steps.instruction: Hang a container from the spile or place below it. Sap flow is highest when nights freeze and days thaw.
steps.helpDetail: Daily yield: 1-3 liters per tap. Birch sap is thinner (less sweet) than maple but flows more abundantly in cold regions.
steps.illustrationDescription: Hang a container from the spil

steps.instruction: Drink sap raw for hydration. It is sterile, mildly sweet, and contains minerals.
steps.helpDetail: Sap provides both hydration and calories. In spring, a well-tapped grove of trees can provide all the water a person needs. Sap ferments within 2-3 days if not consumed.
steps.illustrationDescription: Drink sap raw for hydration. I

techniques.name: Condom Water Carrier
techniques.subtitle: Use a condom as an emergency water container

steps.instruction: An unlubricated condom can hold 1-2 liters of water. Place inside a sock for structural support.
steps.helpDetail: The latex stretches to hold significant volume but is fragile. A sock, bandana, or pant leg provides the structural support the condom lacks.

steps.instruction: Fill slowly to avoid bursting. The condom stretches evenly when filled gradually.
steps.helpDetail: Submerge the opening in water and let it fill naturally, or pour carefully from a cup. Fast filling creates pressure points that burst.

steps.instruction: Tie off the end with a simple overhand knot. Leave enough slack to untie for drinking.
steps.helpDetail: The knot should be secure but not so tight you can't open it. Practice tying and untying before your water source is far away.
steps.illustrationDescription: Tie off the end with a simple

steps.instruction: Carry in a sock or sling made from a shirt sleeve. Never carry by the condom alone.
steps.helpDetail: The latex is tough but punctures easily on branches, rocks, and gear. The fabric carrier protects it during transport.
steps.illustrationDescription: Carry in a sock or sling made

steps.instruction: This also works as a solar still cover, waterproof fire kit storage, or emergency fishing float.
steps.helpDetail: Non-lubricated condoms are one of the most versatile survival items by weight. They are waterproof, elastic, and surprisingly durable when protected.
steps.illustrationDescription: This also works as a solar sti

techniques.name: Mega Rain Catch System
techniques.subtitle: Maximize rainwater collection with large-scale tarp systems

steps.instruction: Stretch the largest available material (tarp, poncho, emergency blanket, trash bags) between trees.
steps.helpDetail: Maximum surface area = maximum water collection. A 6x8 tarp collects ~5 gallons per inch of rain. Angle for drainage.

steps.instruction: Create a funnel point at the lowest corner. Direction all water flow to one collection point.
steps.helpDetail: Twist the corner into a spout and direct into your largest container. Multiple containers staged below catch overflow.
steps.illustrationDescription: Create a funnel point at the l

steps.instruction: Weight the center slightly to create a bowl shape — prevents water from sheeting off the edges.
steps.helpDetail: A small rock in the center creates a sag that pools water before it runs to the funnel point. This captures light drizzle too.

steps.instruction: Channel ground runoff: dig small trenches that route ground-level rainwater to a collection basin.
steps.helpDetail: Ground trenches capture runoff that the overhead tarp misses. Line with clay or plastic if available for impermeable channels.

steps.instruction: First few minutes of rain: let it wash the collection surface clean before collecting for drinking.
steps.helpDetail: The first rain washes dust, bird droppings, and debris off the tarp. Divert the first 2-3 minutes of flow away from your drinking container.
steps.illustrationDescription: First few minutes of rain: let

techniques.name: Fog Net Collection
techniques.subtitle: Harvest water from fog using mesh nets

steps.instruction: Build a frame from sticks or poles, 4-6 feet tall, oriented perpendicular to prevailing wind.
steps.helpDetail: The net must face into the fog/wind. Fog droplets impact the mesh and coalesce into drinkable water. Higher and wider = more collection.
steps.illustrationDescription: Build a frame from sticks or p

steps.instruction: Stretch fine mesh across the frame: mosquito netting, cheesecloth, or tightly woven fabric.
steps.helpDetail: Finer mesh captures smaller fog droplets. Triple-layer netting captures more than single layer. The mesh must allow air to pass through.
steps.illustrationDescription: Stretch fine mesh across the f

steps.instruction: Attach a gutter or channel at the bottom of the mesh to catch dripping water.
steps.helpDetail: A split bamboo section, folded bark, or plastic sheet channels the condensed water to a collection container at one end.
steps.illustrationDescription: Attach a gutter or channel at

steps.instruction: Position on a ridge, hilltop, or coastal bluff where fog is densest.
steps.helpDetail: Fog forms on the windward side of terrain features. Coastal mountains, ridgelines, and passes concentrate fog flow.

steps.instruction: Yield: 2-10 liters per day per square meter of mesh in good fog conditions.
steps.helpDetail: Fog nets provide water in coastal deserts where no other source exists. Used commercially in Chile, Peru, and Morocco.

techniques.name: Vehicle Water Sources
techniques.subtitle: Extract water from a vehicle in an emergency

steps.instruction: Windshield washer fluid: mostly water, but may contain methanol — NOT safe to drink.
steps.helpDetail: DO NOT drink washer fluid. The methanol content (usually 20-50%) causes blindness and death. This is a common fatal mistake.

steps.instruction: Radiator coolant: contains ethylene glycol — LETHAL. Do NOT drink under any circumstances.
steps.helpDetail: Even small amounts cause kidney failure and death. The sweet taste makes it deceptively palatable. Never consume radiator fluid.

steps.instruction: SAFE: condensation from AC system — a running AC drips clean condensate. Collect the drip.
steps.helpDetail: The AC evaporator condenses humidity from the air. The resulting water is distilled-quality. Runs only while the engine is on.

steps.instruction: SAFE: water from the tire — some tires contain water as ballast. Carefully puncture non-critical tires.
steps.helpDetail: Farm equipment, some SUVs, and heavy trucks use calcium chloride water solutions for ballast. Regular car tires typically don't.
steps.illustrationDescription: SAFE: water from the tire — so

steps.instruction: SAFE: solar still using clear car window or windshield glass as a collector over a dug pit.
steps.helpDetail: Remove a window carefully. Use as the cover for a ground solar still — it's better than plastic because glass transmits more UV.

techniques.name: Leaf Funnel Water Collection
techniques.subtitle: Channel rain and dew using large tropical leaves

steps.instruction: Find large, waxy leaves: banana, taro, elephant ear, or any smooth, broad leaf.
steps.helpDetail: Smooth, waxy surfaces channel water efficiently without absorbing it. Fuzzy or hairy leaves absorb water instead of shedding it.

steps.instruction: Fold a large leaf into a funnel shape and secure with a twig or thorn pin.
steps.helpDetail: The narrow end of the funnel directs water into your container. Multiple funnels set up around camp multiply collection.
steps.illustrationDescription: Fold a large leaf into a funne

steps.instruction: Position funnels to catch rain: lean against trees, prop on sticks, or wedge into forked branches.
steps.helpDetail: Angle the funnel so water flows naturally downhill into the container below. The wider the opening, the more rain captured.

steps.instruction: For dew collection: leave funnels out overnight. Dew condenses on the waxy surface and drains to the collection point.
steps.helpDetail: In humid environments, significant dew forms on exposed surfaces. Multiple large-leaf funnels can collect 500mL+ overnight.

steps.instruction: Replace leaves every 2-3 days as they dry out and lose their waxy, waterproof quality.
steps.helpDetail: Fresh leaves channel water best. Dried, curled leaves lose their funnel shape and waterproofness. Always have replacements ready.

techniques.name: Ice Melting for Water
techniques.subtitle: Safely melt ice and snow for drinking water

steps.instruction: NEVER eat snow directly — it lowers core temperature and causes dehydration (your body uses more water to melt it).
steps.helpDetail: Eating snow drops core temp by 1°F per handful. In hypothermia conditions, this is dangerous. Always melt snow before drinking.
steps.illustrationDescription: NEVER eat snow directly — it l

steps.instruction: Choose ice over snow when available — ice yields more water per volume (snow is 90% air).
steps.helpDetail: A pot of snow melts to about 1/10 its volume in water. The same pot of ice produces 9/10 water. Ice is far more efficient.
steps.illustrationDescription: Choose ice over snow when avai

steps.instruction: Start with a small amount of water in the pot, then add snow/ice gradually.
steps.helpDetail: Melting snow in a dry pot can scorch the pot and produce a terrible taste. The initial water accelerates melting.
steps.illustrationDescription: Start with a small amount of w

steps.instruction: Dark-colored containers left in sunlight passively melt snow without fire.
steps.helpDetail: A black bag or dark cloth filled with snow and placed in sun produces water through solar heating. Works even below freezing if the sun is strong.

steps.instruction: Sea ice: old sea ice (blue, rounded, less salty) is drinkable. New sea ice (milky, sharp) is too salty.
steps.helpDetail: Multi-year sea ice has had salt drain out over successive melt cycles. It tastes fresh. First-year ice retains too much salt to drink safely.
steps.illustrationDescription: Sea ice: old sea ice (blue, ro

techniques.name: Bamboo Water Storage
techniques.subtitle: Use bamboo segments as natural water containers

steps.instruction: Cut a bamboo section with a natural node (solid partition) at the bottom — this creates a sealed cup.
steps.helpDetail: Cut just below a node for the bottom seal, and above the next node for the open top. Each segment holds 1-2 cups depending on diameter.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cut a bamboo section with a na

steps.instruction: Green (fresh) bamboo segments may already contain drinkable water — shake to check before cutting.
steps.helpDetail: Rainwater collects in bamboo internodes naturally. If water sloshes when shaken, cut the top open and drink. It's clean and fresh.

steps.instruction: Seal the open top with leaves, clay, or a carved wooden plug for transport.
steps.helpDetail: A tight-fitting plug made from a whittled stick prevents spilling during travel. Carry multiple segments for larger capacity.
steps.illustrationDescription: Seal the open top with leaves,

steps.instruction: For larger storage: don't cut — drill a small hole in the side, fill through the hole, and plug it.
steps.helpDetail: This maintains structural integrity. The natural bamboo wall is stronger when intact. A small plug is easier to seal than an open top.

steps.instruction: Bamboo containers can also be used for boiling water by filling and placing near (not in) the fire.
steps.helpDetail: The water inside prevents the bamboo from igniting. The wall heats the water to boiling. This is a non-metal cooking vessel technique.

techniques.name: Natural Spring Indicators
techniques.subtitle: Find hidden springs using terrain and vegetation clues

steps.instruction: Green vegetation in an otherwise dry area = underground water surfacing.
steps.helpDetail: A line of green trees or bushes in arid terrain marks subsurface water flow. The vegetation taps the water table directly.

steps.instruction: Seepages on hillside slopes, especially at geological layer boundaries (rock type changes).
steps.helpDetail: Water flows through porous rock until it hits impermeable rock. At that boundary, it surfaces. Look for wet rock faces, mossy patches.

steps.instruction: Insect swarms (especially mosquitoes, midges) concentrated in one area indicate moisture beneath.
steps.helpDetail: Insects need water to breed. A persistent cloud of midges over a specific spot often marks a seep or spring.

steps.instruction: Animal trails converging toward a single point — especially at dawn and dusk.
steps.helpDetail: Animals visit water sources at predictable times. Multiple trails converging means a reliable water source at the convergence point.

steps.instruction: Dig at the base of slopes, outside bends of dry creek beds, and near rock outcrops where seeps are likely.
steps.helpDetail: Even when no surface water is visible, digging 1-3 feet at these locations often hits the water table. Let the hole fill over 30 minutes.
steps.illustrationDescription: Dig at the base of slopes, out

techniques.name: Double-Boil Purification
techniques.subtitle: Distill contaminated water using two containers

steps.instruction: Set up: place dirty water in a pot over fire. Cover with an angled lid (metal, flat rock, or bark).
steps.helpDetail: The lid must angle downward toward a collection point. Steam condenses on the lid and runs down the angle into the clean container.
steps.illustrationDescription: Set up: place dirty water in a

steps.instruction: Place a clean collection container at the low edge of the lid to catch condensate drips.
steps.helpDetail: The collection container must be outside the dirty water pot. Position so drops fall cleanly from the lid edge into the clean container.

steps.instruction: Cool the lid from above with a wet cloth or cold water to improve condensation.
steps.helpDetail: A cooler lid surface condenses more steam. Periodically wet a cloth on top of the lid, or pile snow/ice on it in cold conditions.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cool the lid from above with a

steps.instruction: Bring the dirty water to a steady simmer — not a rolling boil. Excess heat wastes fuel and reduces condensation.
steps.helpDetail: A gentle simmer produces steady steam without energy waste. The goal is consistent condensation, not rapid boiling.
steps.illustrationDescription: Bring the dirty water to a ste

steps.instruction: This produces DISTILLED water — safe from biological AND chemical contaminants.
steps.helpDetail: Unlike simple boiling (which kills pathogens but leaves chemicals), distillation removes everything. The condensate is pure H2O. Slower but safer.

techniques.name: Gypsy Well (Seep Hole)
techniques.subtitle: Extract filtered groundwater near dry riverbeds

steps.instruction: Locate a dry riverbed or a depression at the base of a slope.
steps.helpDetail: Look for green vegetation, damp sand, or places where water naturally channels during rain. The outside bend of a dry creek is ideal.
steps.illustrationDescription: Locate a dry riverbed or a dep

steps.instruction: Dig a hole 2 to 3 feet deep.
steps.helpDetail: If you hit wet mud or water begins to seep in, stop digging. If it is completely bone dry after 3 feet, move to another location.
steps.illustrationDescription: Dig a hole 2 to 3 feet deep.

steps.instruction: Let the hole sit undisturbed for 1 to 2 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Muddy water will slowly seep into the hole from the surrounding water table. Do not try to scoop it out immediately.
steps.illustrationDescription: Let the hole sit undisturbed f

steps.instruction: Line the sides of the hole with rocks or wooden stakes.
steps.helpDetail: This prevents the sandy walls from caving in and dirtying the water further.
steps.illustrationDescription: Line the sides of the hole wit

steps.instruction: Carefully scoop the water out of the top layer.
steps.helpDetail: The dirt will have settled to the bottom. Even though it is ground-filtered, you must still boil or chemically purify this water before drinking.

techniques.name: Pit Solar Still
techniques.subtitle: Extract water from dirt and vegetation using evaporation

steps.instruction: Dig a pit roughly 3 feet wide and 2 feet deep in an area that receives full sunlight.
steps.helpDetail: The wider and deeper the pit, the more surface area you have to extract water from.
steps.illustrationDescription: Dig a pit roughly 3 feet wide

steps.instruction: Place a clean catch container (cup, bottle, bowl) in the exact center of the pit.
steps.helpDetail: If you have a drinking tube (like a hydration bladder hose), run it from the cup up and out of the pit so you can drink without disassembling the still.

steps.instruction: Fill the empty space around the cup with chopped green vegetation or safely contaminated water.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT use poisonous plants. The sun will bake the moisture out of the leaves/dirt.
steps.illustrationDescription: Fill the empty space around th

steps.instruction: Cover the pit with a large sheet of clear plastic.
steps.helpDetail: Seal the edges completely with dirt and rocks. No air can escape.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cover the pit with a large she

steps.instruction: Place a small rock in the center of the plastic, directly above the cup.
steps.helpDetail: The plastic should form an inverted cone. Moisture will evaporate, condense on the underside of the plastic, and run down to drip into the cup.
steps.illustrationDescription: Place a small rock in the cent

techniques.name: Safe Cactus Hydration
techniques.subtitle: Myths and realities of getting water from cacti

steps.instruction: WARNING: Most cacti are toxic and will cause severe vomiting, accelerating dehydration.
steps.helpDetail: The 'barrel cactus' myth is dangerous. Only the Fishhook Barrel Cactus is marginally safe, and only in extreme desperation.

steps.instruction: Instead of barrel cacti, look for Prickly Pear (Opuntia) fruit or pads.
steps.helpDetail: The fruit (tunas) and young pads (nopales) hold significant safe moisture. Burn off the microscopic spines (glochids) over a fire first.

steps.instruction: If near Agave plants, you can harvest the flower stalk.
steps.helpDetail: Before the stalk blooms, you can cut the central shoot. The plant will seep a sugary sap into the depression over several days.
steps.illustrationDescription: If near Agave plants, you can

steps.instruction: Do NOT drink milky sap.
steps.helpDetail: Any desert plant that bleeds a white, milky sap (like Euphorbia) is highly toxic and will burn your throat and stomach.

steps.instruction: Rely on solar stills or animal trails rather than cracking open random cacti.
steps.helpDetail: Cactus fluid is largely composed of malic acid and toxic alkaloids that stress the kidneys.
steps.illustrationDescription: Rely on solar stills or animal

techniques.name: Birch and Maple Tapping
techniques.subtitle: Harvesting safe, hydrating tree sap in springtime

steps.instruction: This method only works in late Winter or early Spring, when daytime temps are above freezing and nights are below freezing.
steps.helpDetail: During this time, sap flows upward heavily to feed new buds.
steps.illustrationDescription: This method only works in late

steps.instruction: Identify a Sugar Maple, Silver Maple, or Birch tree.
steps.helpDetail: They must be mature (at least 10 inches in diameter).

steps.instruction: Drill or carve a hole 2 inches deep into the trunk at a slight upward angle.
steps.helpDetail: The hole should be about breast height.
steps.illustrationDescription: Drill or carve a hole 2 inches

steps.instruction: Insert a hollow spile.
steps.helpDetail: You can carve a spile out of elderberry wood (by pushing out the pith) or fold a piece of metal. Tap it gently into the hole.

steps.instruction: Hang a container below the spile.
steps.helpDetail: The sap will drip slowly. Birch and Maple sap is essentially pure, slightly sweet water that can be drank straight from the tree without purification.
steps.illustrationDescription: Hang a container below the spi

techniques.name: Hot Rock Boiling (Wooden Bowl)
techniques.subtitle: Boiling water without a metal container

steps.instruction: Find a natural depression in a log, or carve/burn a bowl into heavy piece of wood.
steps.helpDetail: Alternatively, construct a pit lined with a dense, waterproof clay or an animal hide.
steps.illustrationDescription: Find a natural depression in a

steps.instruction: Build a large fire and heat several fist-sized, dry, non-porous rocks.
steps.helpDetail: NEVER use rocks from a riverbed, as the trapped moisture will turn to steam and the rock will explode like a hand grenade. Use dry upland rocks (quartz/granite).
steps.illustrationDescription: Build a large fire and heat se

steps.instruction: Fill your wooden bowl or hide with the dirty water.
steps.helpDetail: Do not overfill; leave room for the displacement of the rocks.
steps.illustrationDescription: Fill your wooden bowl or hide

steps.instruction: Using green wooden tongs, transfer a glowing hot rock from the fire into the water.
steps.helpDetail: The water will instantly hiss and begin to boil.

steps.instruction: Rotate rocks as they cool.
steps.helpDetail: Pull the cool rock out, put a fresh hot rock in. Keep adding hot rocks until the water achieves a rolling boil for 1 full minute.

techniques.name: Solar Disinfection (SODIS)
techniques.subtitle: Using UV radiation from sunlight to kill pathogens in clear water

steps.instruction: Fill a clean, clear PET plastic bottle (like a soda bottle) with water.
steps.helpDetail: The water MUST be clear. If turbid, pre-filter it through cloth or sand first. UV cannot penetrate murky water.
steps.illustrationDescription: Fill a clean, clear PET plasti

steps.instruction: Lay the bottle on its side on a dark, reflective surface in direct sunlight.
steps.helpDetail: A dark rock or corrugated metal roof is ideal. The reflected heat from below assists the UV penetration.
steps.illustrationDescription: Lay the bottle on its side on

steps.instruction: Leave it exposed for a minimum of 6 hours of strong, direct sunlight.
steps.helpDetail: If the sky is overcast, extend the exposure to 48 hours.
steps.illustrationDescription: Leave it exposed for a minimum

steps.instruction: UV-A radiation destroys the DNA of bacteria, viruses, and protozoa.
steps.helpDetail: This method is WHO-endorsed and used by millions in developing countries daily.

steps.instruction: Drink directly from the bottle.
steps.helpDetail: Do not pour it into a contaminated container afterward or you will recontaminate it.

techniques.name: DIY Charcoal Water Filter
techniques.subtitle: Building a multi-layer gravity filter from natural materials

steps.instruction: Cut the bottom off a large plastic bottle or use a hollow log section.
steps.helpDetail: Poke a small drain hole in the cap (if using a bottle) and invert it so the cap is at the bottom.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cut the bottom off a large pla

steps.instruction: Layer the filter materials from bottom to top: fine sand, crushed charcoal, coarse sand, gravel, grass.
steps.helpDetail: Each layer should be 2–3 inches thick.

steps.instruction: The charcoal is the critical layer.
steps.helpDetail: Make charcoal by burning hardwood to black coals, dousing them in water, then crushing them into pea-sized granules. Charcoal absorbs chemicals, heavy metals, and bad taste.
steps.illustrationDescription: The charcoal is the critical l

steps.instruction: Pour dirty water into the top of the filter.
steps.helpDetail: Let gravity pull it through. The first several passes will be black from charcoal dust—this is normal. Keep running water through until it runs clear.
steps.illustrationDescription: Pour dirty water into the top

steps.instruction: CRITICAL: This filter removes sediment and improves taste, but does NOT kill bacteria or viruses.
steps.helpDetail: You must STILL boil or chemically treat the filtered water before drinking.

techniques.name: Seawater Distillation
techniques.subtitle: Converting saltwater to freshwater using heat

steps.instruction: NEVER drink seawater directly. The salt concentration (3.5%) accelerates dehydration and causes kidney failure.
steps.helpDetail: You must distill it by evaporating the water and condensing the steam.

steps.instruction: Boil seawater in a pot or metal container.
steps.helpDetail: If you don't have a pot, use the hot-rock boiling method in a hollowed log.
steps.illustrationDescription: Boil seawater in a pot or meta

steps.instruction: Capture the steam.
steps.helpDetail: Place a clean cloth, T-shirt, or piece of metal angled above the boiling pot. Steam rises, hits the cooler surface, and condenses into freshwater droplets.

steps.instruction: Channel the condensed droplets into a catch container.
steps.helpDetail: Angle the condensation surface so the drops roll downhill into a cup placed below the edge.

steps.instruction: The collected water is pure H2O, free of salt and pathogens.
steps.helpDetail: It will taste flat. Add a tiny pinch of wood ash for minerals and improved taste.
steps.illustrationDescription: The collected water is pure H2

techniques.name: Iodine Water Purification
techniques.subtitle: Chemical disinfection using iodine tablets or tincture

steps.instruction: Add 5 drops of 2% iodine tincture per liter of clear water.
steps.helpDetail: If the water is cloudy, double the dose to 10 drops.

steps.instruction: If using iodine tablets (like Potable Aqua), add 2 tablets per liter.
steps.helpDetail: Pop the cap slightly loose and invert the bottle to let treated water seep into the threads, disinfecting the cap area.

steps.instruction: Wait 30 minutes before drinking.
steps.helpDetail: In cold water (below 40°F), wait 60 minutes. Cold dramatically slows the disinfection chemical reaction.

steps.instruction: The water will taste slightly of iodine.
